content: P/E ratio = Price / Earnings Per Share. Trailing P/E uses last 12 months earnings, forward P/E uses analyst estimates. S&P 500 historical average: 20-22x. P/E > 30 may indicate overvaluation unless justified by high growth.

PEG ratio = P/E / earnings growth rate. PEG < 1.0 suggests undervaluation relative to growth. PEG = 1.0 is fair value. PEG > 2.0 is expensive. More useful than raw P/E for comparing growth stocks with different growth rates.

EV/EBITDA = Enterprise Value / EBITDA. Enterprise Value = Market Cap + Debt - Cash. Sector benchmarks: Tech 15-25x, Healthcare 12-18x, Industrials 8-12x, Utilities 8-10x. Preferred over P/E because it's capital-structure neutral.

Free Cash Flow Yield = FCF / Market Cap. FCF = Operating Cash Flow - CapEx. Yield > 5% is attractive. > 8% may indicate undervaluation or declining business. Compare to treasury yield — FCF yield should exceed risk-free rate by 3%+.

DuPont ROE decomposition: ROE = Net Margin × Asset Turnover × Equity Multiplier = (NI/Revenue) × (Revenue/Assets) × (Assets/Equity). Identifies whether high ROE comes from profitability, efficiency, or leverage. Leverage-driven ROE is riskier.

ROIC = NOPAT / Invested Capital = Operating Income × (1-Tax Rate) / (Total Assets - Current Liabilities - Cash). ROIC > WACC creates shareholder value. ROIC < WACC destroys value. Consistent ROIC > 15% indicates strong competitive moat.

DCF valuation: Value = sum(FCF_t / (1+WACC)^t) + Terminal Value / (1+WACC)^n. Terminal Value = FCF_n × (1+g) / (WACC-g) (Gordon Growth). Terminal value often accounts for 60-80% of total value. Always run sensitivity analysis on WACC and growth rate.

Earnings quality indicators: Operating Cash Flow / Net Income > 1.0 suggests high quality (cash backs up earnings). Accruals ratio = (Net Income - OCF) / Total Assets. High accruals (> 10%) predict future earnings decline and stock underperformance.

Sector rotation by economic cycle: Early Recovery → Consumer Discretionary, Financials. Mid Expansion → Technology, Industrials. Late Cycle → Energy, Materials, Healthcare. Recession → Utilities, Consumer Staples, Gold. ISM PMI is the key cycle indicator.

GDP growth rate: real GDP growth > 3% = strong expansion, 1-3% = moderate, 0-1% = stagnation, negative = recession. US averages ~2% long-term. GDP is lagging indicator — PMI and jobless claims lead by 3-6 months.

CPI (Consumer Price Index) measures headline inflation. Core CPI excludes food and energy. Fed targets 2% PCE inflation (slightly different from CPI). CPI > 4% typically triggers rate hikes. CPI surprises move bond yields and rate-sensitive sectors.

Non-Farm Payrolls (NFP): monthly employment report, usually first Friday. NFP > 200K = strong labor market. < 100K = weakening. Released 8:30 AM ET, causes high volatility in forex/bonds/equities for first 30 minutes. Trade after initial reaction settles.

PMI (Purchasing Managers Index): > 50 = expansion, < 50 = contraction. ISM Manufacturing PMI is the gold standard. PMI leads GDP by 1-2 quarters. New Orders sub-index is most forward-looking. PMI consistently < 45 signals recession risk.

Yield curve: 2s10s spread (10Y minus 2Y Treasury). Normal = positive (long rates > short). Inverted = negative (recession signal with ~18 month lead). The yield curve has predicted every US recession since 1970 with one false signal.

Fed Funds Rate impact: rate hikes hurt growth/tech stocks (higher discount rate reduces present value of future earnings), benefit banks (wider net interest margin). Rate cuts do the opposite. Watch the dot plot and Fed funds futures for expectations.

Post-earnings drift: stocks that beat estimates tend to drift up for 60 days, misses drift down. The initial reaction captures only ~50% of the total move. Strategy: buy stocks that beat by > 10% with positive guidance, hold 30-60 days.

Insider trading signals (SEC Form 4): cluster buying by 3+ insiders within 30 days is strongly bullish (outperforms by 7-13% annually). CEO/CFO purchases are most significant. Insider selling is less informative (could be diversification/liquidity).

Short interest as percentage of float: > 20% = heavily shorted, squeeze potential. Days to cover = short shares / avg daily volume. > 10 days = high squeeze risk. Track changes: rapidly increasing shorts = bearish conviction growing.

Dividend yield = Annual Dividend / Stock Price. Dividend aristocrats: S&P 500 companies with 25+ consecutive years of dividend increases. Payout ratio = Dividends / Earnings. Payout > 80% is unsustainable for most companies. Target 30-60% for stability.

Current ratio = Current Assets / Current Liabilities. > 2.0 = strong liquidity, 1.0-2.0 = adequate, < 1.0 = potential liquidity crisis. Quick ratio excludes inventory: (Cash + Receivables) / Current Liabilities. More conservative measure.

Debt/Equity ratio: < 0.5 = conservative, 0.5-1.0 = moderate, > 2.0 = highly leveraged. Interest coverage = EBIT / Interest Expense. Coverage < 2x = danger zone, may struggle to service debt. Critical for credit-sensitive sectors like REITs and utilities.

Revenue growth rate: organic (excluding M&A) growth > 15% = high growth, 5-15% = moderate, < 5% = mature. Compare to industry average. Decelerating growth even at high levels can trigger multiple compression (P/E shrinks).

Margin analysis: gross margin shows product economics, operating margin shows operational efficiency, net margin shows bottom-line profitability. Expanding margins = operating leverage. Contracting margins = competitive pressure or input cost inflation.

Competitive moat types (Morningstar framework): brand (Apple), network effects (Visa), switching costs (Salesforce), cost advantage (Walmart), efficient scale (natural monopolies like utilities). Companies with wide moats sustain ROIC > WACC for decades.

P/B ratio = Price / Book Value per share. Below 1.0 may indicate undervaluation (stock trading below liquidation value) or declining business. Financial stocks use P/B heavily. Tech stocks have low book value, making P/B less useful.

OpenBB Platform (65K GitHub stars) provides unified access to 30+ financial data providers. Stocks, options, crypto, economics, forex. Python SDK replaces fragile Yahoo Finance scraping. ROOT should use OpenBB as its primary data layer.

FinBERT: BERT model fine-tuned on financial text for sentiment analysis. Faster than LLM-based sentiment and runs locally without API cost. Classifies financial text as positive/negative/neutral with ~85% accuracy on financial datasets.

SEC EDGAR filings via edgartools Python library: access 10-K (annual), 10-Q (quarterly), 8-K (current events), 13-F (institutional holdings), Form 4 (insider trades). XBRL parsing for automated financial statement analysis.

TradingAgents framework (48K stars): multi-agent LLM trading system mirroring real firms with specialized agents: Fundamental Analyst, Sentiment Analyst, News Analyst, Technical Analyst, Researcher, Trader, Risk Manager. Validates ROOT's multi-agent approach.

Behavioral finance biases: loss aversion (losses feel 2.5x worse than equivalent gains), anchoring (fixating on purchase price), herding (following crowd during bubbles/panics), disposition effect (selling winners too early, holding losers too long).

Confirmation bias in trading: seeking information that confirms existing position while ignoring contradictory evidence. Counter: maintain a kill thesis for every position — write down what would make you wrong and check it regularly.

Market microstructure: Level 2 shows full order book (bid/ask at each price level). Dark pools execute ~40% of US equity volume off-exchange. Payment for order flow means retail orders rarely get best execution. Use limit orders, not market orders.

Alpaca paper trading API provides free simulated trading with real market data. Use for strategy validation before risking capital. WebSocket streaming for real-time quotes. Python SDK: alpaca-py. ROOT should validate all signals via paper trading first.

Walk-forward optimization: optimize strategy parameters on training window (e.g., 6 months), test on out-of-sample window (e.g., 2 months), roll forward and repeat. Prevents overfitting that plagues in-sample-only backtests.

Freqtrade (48K stars) strategy class pattern: populate_indicators() adds technical indicators, populate_entry_trend() defines buy conditions, populate_exit_trend() defines sell conditions. Clean separation of concerns for strategy development.

FinRL (15K stars): deep reinforcement learning for trading. Implements DRL agents (A2C, DDPG, PPO, SAC, TD3) for stock trading and portfolio allocation. Ensemble approach (combining multiple DRL agents) consistently outperforms individual models.

Factor investing: Fama-French 5 factors — market (beta), size (small minus big), value (high minus low book/market), profitability (robust minus weak), investment (conservative minus aggressive). Multi-factor portfolios reduce concentration risk.

Quantitative alpha generation: combine signals from different categories (momentum, mean-reversion, fundamental, sentiment, alternative data). Orthogonal signals add diversification. Use rolling IC (Information Coefficient) to weight signals adaptively.

Earnings surprise magnitude matters: beats by > 10% with raised guidance = strongest signal. Beats by < 5% may already be priced in. Track analyst revision momentum — stocks with rising estimates outperform those with falling estimates.

Qlib (Microsoft, 40K stars): AI-oriented quantitative investment platform. ML research pipeline with DataServer 10x faster than Pandas for time-series. Built-in models (LSTM, LightGBM, Transformer). ROOT should integrate for ML-based alpha generation.

TimeGPT (Nixtla): pre-trained foundation model for time series forecasting trained on 100B+ data points. Few lines of code for price forecasting without training. API-based, easy integration. Could provide instant forecasting for ROOT's hedge fund.

Regime detection using Hidden Markov Models: identify trending vs mean-reverting vs high-volatility market states. Use different strategies per regime — momentum in trends, mean-reversion in ranging, reduced exposure in high-vol. HMM with 3 states is standard.

Dollar-Cost Averaging (DCA) reduces timing risk: invest fixed amount at regular intervals regardless of price. Mathematically suboptimal vs lump sum (markets trend up ~70% of years), but psychologically easier and reduces regret from poor timing.

Market cap categories: Mega cap > $200B (AAPL, MSFT), Large cap $10-200B, Mid cap $2-10B, Small cap $300M-2B, Micro cap < $300M. Smaller caps have higher expected returns but higher volatility and lower liquidity. Size premium = ~2% annually.

Calendar effects: January effect (small caps outperform in January, weaker in recent years), Sell in May (May-Oct returns lower than Nov-Apr historically), Santa Rally (last 5 trading days + first 2 of new year positive ~75% of time).

Crypto on-chain analysis: monitor whale wallet movements (>1000 BTC), exchange inflows (selling pressure) vs outflows (accumulation), active addresses (network usage), hash rate (mining security), stablecoin supply on exchanges (dry powder for buying).

DEX mechanics: Automated Market Makers (AMMs) use constant product formula x*y=k (Uniswap V2). Concentrated liquidity (V3) allows LPs to specify price ranges for better capital efficiency. Slippage increases with trade size relative to pool depth.

CCXT library (42K stars): unified crypto exchange API covering 100+ exchanges (Binance, Coinbase, Kraken, etc). Single Python interface for order placement, market data, account management. Essential if ROOT expands to crypto trading.

Revenue from trading data: package ROOT's signal generation as API ($29-99/mo), market sentiment dashboard, prediction accuracy leaderboard leveraging Prediction Ledger. Data products have 80-90% margins after initial build.
